name: 3d-animation
title: Purposeful 3D Animation
useWhen: A car, object, or interface benefits from depth
A hero requires an interactive 3D reveal
description: Designs restrained, performant 3D interactions with explicit mobile and reduced-motion fallbacks.

markdownSections.trigger: Use only when 3D materially improves product understanding, brand experience, or interaction.
markdownSections.mission: Create a technically feasible 3D interaction plan without sacrificing readability, performance, or accessibility.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Device targets, performance budget, animation stack, scene complexity, lighting, camera, loading strategy, mobile support, and whether 2D is stronger.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Define the purpose of 3D.
2. Choose Three.js, React Three Fiber, Spline, CSS 3D, or pre-rendered media.
3. Create a scene graph.
4. Define camera and lighting.
5. Define pointer and scroll input.
6. Budget polygons, textures, draw calls, and bundle size.
7. Create fallbacks.
8. Test loading and frame rate.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- Do not add 3D tilt to every card.
- Do not block content behind WebGL.
- Do not require gyroscope permission.
- Do not ship oversized scenes.
- Do not use 3D when layered 2D motion is cleaner.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Scene concept
- Tool choice
- Asset budget
- Interaction timeline
- Fallback plan
- Performance targets
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Scene has clear purpose.
2. Content remains accessible without 3D.
3. Mobile and reduced-motion fallbacks exist.
4. Critical content is not blocked.
5. Frame rate is acceptable on mid-range devices.
6. Scene resources clean up correctly.

examplesMarkdown:
# Purposeful 3D Animation Examples

## Simple Request
Use `3d-animation` to use 3d-animation to render an interactive spinning logo using canvas.

## Advanced Request
Use `3d-animation` to use 3d-animation to build a headlight-to-car reveal with a fallback image for mobile and reduced-motion states.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `3d-animation` to do not use 3d-animation to implement simple button hover animations.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use 3d-animation to create the WebGL hero scene, asset-optimizer to compress texture files, and performance-optimizer to limit frame loop lag.
```

name: accessibility-auditor
title: Accessibility Auditing
useWhen: After adding drawers, menus, or modals
avoidWhen: Relying only on automated testing tools
Removing focus outlines to improve aesthetic look
description: Audits keyboard, focus, semantics, contrast, motion, touch, and assistive-technology behavior.

markdownSections.trigger: Use before release and for healthcare or interaction-heavy work.
markdownSections.mission: Find and fix barriers without stripping visual identity.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Headings, landmarks, links, buttons, dialogs, focus order, focus trap, Escape, contrast, touch targets, alt text, duplicate text, scroll traps, live regions, forms, and reduced motion.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Run automated checks.
2. Perform keyboard review.
3. Review dialogs and drawers.
4. Review motion and scroll.
5. Review touch targets.
6. Review contrast.
7. Document severity and fixes.
8. Re-test.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- Automated checks are not enough.
- Do not remove focus outlines.
- Use `aria-hidden` rather than deleting useful decorative text.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Severity-ranked findings
- Fix list
- Keyboard results
- Reduced-motion results
- Exceptions
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Core tasks work by keyboard.
2. Focus is visible.
3. Drawers trap and restore focus.
4. Reduced motion works.
5. Important content is announced.
6. Touch controls are usable.

examplesMarkdown:
# Accessibility Auditing Examples

## Simple Request
Use `accessibility-auditor` to use accessibility-auditor to audit page image alt attributes.

## Advanced Request
Use `accessibility-auditor` to use accessibility-auditor to verify dialog focus traps, contrast, Escape handling, and reduced-motion stylesheet mappings.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `accessibility-auditor` to do not use accessibility-auditor to change visual typography font styles.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use accessibility-auditor to identify tab order problems, component-refactor to clean up markup, and interaction-qa-agent to double-check.
```

name: asset-optimizer
title: Asset Optimization
useWhen: Portraits download at desktop sizes on phones
avoidWhen: Compressing assets until they degrade or blur
description: Optimizes images, video, fonts, and visual assets for responsive delivery.

markdownSections.trigger: Use when the site has large screenshots, portraits, project posters, video, or multiple fonts.
markdownSections.mission: Create an asset inventory and delivery strategy balancing quality, crop, format, and loading priority.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Dimensions, formats, transparency, compression, responsive variants, Next Image sizes, preload, poster frames, SVG cleanliness, and duplicates.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Inventory assets.
2. Classify critical and deferred media.
3. Create responsive variants.
4. Convert formats where useful.
5. Define `sizes`.
6. Preload only critical assets.
7. Verify crops.
8. Measure savings.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- Do not share font files.
- Do not degrade logo edges.
- Do not preload every project image.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Asset inventory
- Conversion plan
- Responsive map
- Preload plan
- Savings report
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Correct asset loads per breakpoint.
2. No accidental crop changes.
3. Critical visuals remain sharp.
4. Duplicates are removed.
5. Transferred bytes decrease.

examplesMarkdown:
# Asset Optimization Examples

## Simple Request
Use `asset-optimizer` to use asset-optimizer to compress a folder of images to WebP.

## Advanced Request
Use `asset-optimizer` to use asset-optimizer to clean noisy vector SVGs, convert imagery to AVIF responsive variants, and subset Punjabi fonts.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `asset-optimizer` to do not use asset-optimizer to change card aspect ratios in HTML/CSS.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use media-fit-specialist to define layouts, asset-optimizer to generate compressed source sets, and performance-optimizer to review load speeds.
```

name: automotive-ux
title: Automotive UX Design
useWhen: Structuring ceramic coating or PPF page layouts
avoidWhen: Showing cars without local context or proof
description: Designs premium automotive detailing websites around trust, craftsmanship, proof, and booking intent.

markdownSections.trigger: Use for House of Details or similar automotive service businesses.
markdownSections.mission: Balance cinematic premium presentation with clear services, packages, proof, location, and inquiry flow.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Service taxonomy, package comparison, before/after assets, trust signals, location, WhatsApp/call flows, pricing clarity, mobile audience, and local SEO.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Establish premium positioning.
2. Clarify services and packages.
3. Prioritize proof.
4. Make booking and WhatsApp visible.
5. Add craftsmanship evidence.
6. Optimize mobile discovery.
7. Validate local search structure.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- No generic gold luxury styling.
- Do not hide practical information behind animation.
- Do not overuse car imagery without proof.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- UX audit
- Service architecture
- Booking flow
- Proof strategy
- Mobile CTA plan
- Local SEO notes
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Services are quickly understandable.
2. Booking path is obvious.
3. Proof is credible.
4. Design remains restrained.
5. Mobile conversion is low-friction.

## Advanced Request

## Incorrect Use


name: backend-admin-preserver
title: Backend & Admin Preservation
useWhen: Doctors or timings come from admin
avoidWhen: Replacing backend content with static arrays
description: Protects CMS, Django Admin, database, and editable content flows during redesigns.

markdownSections.trigger: Use when content is managed through an admin panel or backend.
markdownSections.mission: Ensure visual changes do not hard-code or bypass content that must remain editable.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Models, serializers, templates, APIs, media storage, admin fields, validation, permissions, caching, and content dependencies.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Map editable data.
2. Identify frontend consumers.
3. Preserve field contracts.
4. Add fields only when justified.
5. Provide migration and fallback behavior.
6. Test admin edits through frontend.
7. Verify permissions.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- Do not replace backend data with static arrays.
- Do not expose admin routes.
- Do not break existing content during schema change.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Data-flow map
- Fields preserved or added
- Migration notes
- Admin test results
- Fallback behavior
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Existing content still renders.
2. New design remains editable.
3. Admin changes appear correctly.
4. Permissions remain intact.
5. No migration loses content.

examplesMarkdown:
# Backend & Admin Preservation Examples

## Simple Request
Use `backend-admin-preserver` to use backend-admin-preserver to view active database model fields.

## Advanced Request
Use `backend-admin-preserver` to use backend-admin-preserver to map OPD models and API hooks, ensuring admin changes propagate correctly to frontend sections.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `backend-admin-preserver` to do not use backend-admin-preserver to write custom visual styling classes.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use backend-admin-preserver to map database routes, component-refactor to rewrite visual templates, and interaction-qa-agent to verify admin edits.
```

name: codebase-recon
title: Codebase Reconnaissance
avoidWhen: Modifying code during inspection unless explicitly requested
description: Maps an unfamiliar website codebase before design or implementation begins.

markdownSections.trigger: Use at the start of any non-trivial website task.
markdownSections.mission: Produce a reliable map of framework, routes, components, styles, state, animation, data sources, and deployment structure.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Package files, routing, root layout, pages, components, styles, animation libraries, smooth scroll, data modules, backend, tests, environment, and deployment metadata.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Identify framework and versions.
2. Map routes.
3. Map shared and page components.
4. Locate tokens and global CSS.
5. Locate data sources.
6. Locate animation systems.
7. Identify brittle and duplicated logic.
8. Produce a change-risk map.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- Do not modify code during reconnaissance unless requested.
- Do not assume production matches local or main.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Architecture map
- Route map
- Component map
- Library inventory
- Data-flow map
- Risk list
- Recommended agents
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Another agent can work without rediscovery.
2. Shared and page components are distinguished.
3. Deployment drift is identified.
4. Animation and scroll systems are mapped.
markdownSections.handoff: Route to the smallest relevant specialist set.

examplesMarkdown:
# Codebase Reconnaissance Examples

## Simple Request
Use `codebase-recon` to use codebase-recon to list files in the project folder.

## Advanced Request
Use `codebase-recon` to use codebase-recon to map routing, global styles, animation loaders, and Django integration in an unfamiliar CMS workspace.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `codebase-recon` to do not use codebase-recon to rewrite or debug source code files.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use codebase-recon to map out visual selectors, designer-mobile to make edits, and visual-qa-agent to confirm formatting.
```

name: component-refactor
title: Component Refactoring
useWhen: One component controls the entire homepage
avoidWhen: Refactoring unrelated backend code during visual fixes
description: Refactors brittle page code into reusable, testable, data-driven components.

markdownSections.trigger: Use when CSS is scattered, project rules are hard-coded, or one component controls too much.
markdownSections.mission: Reduce magic numbers and duplication without erasing intentional project-specific behavior.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Component boundaries, props, data structures, CSS ownership, repeated markup, animation ownership, state, and responsive branches.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Identify repeated patterns.
2. Separate data from presentation.
3. Create stable component APIs.
4. Centralize tokens and per-project config.
5. Preserve semantic markup.
6. Update tests and screenshots.
7. Confirm no regressions.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- Do not over-abstract one-off editorial work.
- Do not create components with dozens of booleans.
- Do not refactor unrelated backend code.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Component plan
- New APIs
- Files changed
- Removed duplication
- Regression screenshots
- Migration notes
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Components have clear responsibility.
2. Data-driven differences remain possible.
3. Animation cleanup is local.
4. Responsive logic is understandable.
5. Visual output remains stable.

examplesMarkdown:
# Component Refactoring Examples

## Simple Request
Use `component-refactor` to use component-refactor to extract a simple list item into its own component.

## Advanced Request
Use `component-refactor` to use component-refactor to split a giant home script into data-driven ProjectCards and a separate layout shell.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `component-refactor` to do not use component-refactor to add unrelated backend logic to the UI.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use codebase-recon to identify brittle layouts, component-refactor to separate styling, and interaction-qa-agent to test click flows.
```

name: content-hierarchy
title: Content Hierarchy Optimization
useWhen: Pages feel long but say little
description: Reduces repetition and reorganizes content around trust, evidence, understanding, and action.

markdownSections.trigger: Use when pages feel crowded, repetitive, empty, or unclear despite good visuals.
markdownSections.mission: Decide what belongs on each route and section so users understand the offer and act quickly.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Repeated content, service explanations, credentials, CTAs, page purpose, scan behavior, trust needs, and conversion paths.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Define each page’s job.
2. Rank content by user need.
3. Remove repetition.
4. Move detail to correct routes.
5. Preserve proof and credibility.
6. Define concise section copy.
7. Validate scanning order.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- Do not add filler to occupy negative space.
- Do not repeat full case studies on the homepage.
- Do not hide emergency or operational information.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Page-purpose map
- Keep/remove/move table
- Section order
- Copy limits
- CTA map
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Each section has one job.
2. Repetition is removed.
3. Important proof remains.
4. CTAs appear logically.
5. Pages are shorter without losing meaning.
markdownSections.handoff: Use a domain UX agent and `design-me`.

examplesMarkdown:
# Content Hierarchy Optimization Examples

## Simple Request
Use `content-hierarchy` to use content-hierarchy to reduce duplicate text blocks in headers.

## Advanced Request
Use `content-hierarchy` to use content-hierarchy to create a clear layout structure mapping trust elements, services comparison, and CTA actions on the homepage.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `content-hierarchy` to do not use content-hierarchy to write raw SEO-optimized copy for ranking.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use content-hierarchy to define section mapping, design-me to shape typography, and ux-conversion-strategist to position buttons.
```

name: deployment-verifier
title: Deployment Verification
useWhen: Build succeeded but behavior is wrong
avoidWhen: Declaring fixed without checking live URL
description: Verifies Git branch, production deployment, domain alias, logs, cache, and live-site behavior.

markdownSections.trigger: Use whenever local and live versions differ or before declaring release complete.
markdownSections.mission: Prove that the intended commit is serving on the intended domain and behaves correctly.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Git commit, production branch, deployment status, domain assignment, build logs, environment variables, runtime errors, caching, asset paths, and live screenshots.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Identify expected commit.
2. Identify production project and domain.
3. Verify deployment status.
4. Inspect build and runtime logs.
5. Compare live and local screenshots.
6. Test critical routes.
7. Bypass stale cache where needed.
8. Report exact production state.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- Do not assume local build means production is correct.
- Do not infer live code from one stale fetch without deployment verification.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Production verification
- Commit and deployment identifiers
- Domain mapping
- Log findings
- Live screenshots
- Remaining risks
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Correct commit is live.
2. Domain points to correct project.
3. Build succeeds.
4. Critical routes work.
5. No severe runtime errors.
6. Live screenshots match expected design.
markdownSections.handoff: Return production differences to the responsible implementation agent.

examplesMarkdown:
# Deployment Verification Examples

## Simple Request
Use `deployment-verifier` to use deployment-verifier to check Vercel build status.

## Advanced Request
Use `deployment-verifier` to use deployment-verifier to inspect production aliases, env keys, error logs, and verify server responses on live routes.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `deployment-verifier` to do not use deployment-verifier to design responsive desktop layouts.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use any design skill to make layout adjustments, run building tools, and deployment-verifier to verify correct commit changes are live.
```

name: design-me
title: Creative Design Audit
description: Audits and redesigns websites while preserving the strongest parts of their identity.

markdownSections.trigger: Use for a redesign, premium visual direction, hierarchy correction, or full-page critique.
markdownSections.mission: Act as lead creative director. Identify why the site feels weak, separate structural failures from decoration, and create an implementation-ready design direction.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Live routes, component hierarchy, typography, spacing, colour, cards, motion, fixed controls, mobile states, content repetition, and baseline screenshots.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Define the site’s business goal and audience.
2. Identify the intended visual personality.
3. Separate strong existing elements from weak ones.
4. Rank defects by user impact.
5. Define typography, spacing, colour, card, and motion systems.
6. Redesign only necessary sections.
7. Produce a scoped implementation brief.
8. Validate desktop and mobile screenshots.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- Do not clone references pixel-for-pixel.
- Do not add effects to hide weak hierarchy.
- Do not redesign every page at once.
- Do not introduce a new palette without reason.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Design audit
- Prioritized defects
- Revised section architecture
- Design tokens
- Component instructions
- Acceptance criteria
- Before/after screenshots
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Primary hierarchy is obvious within five seconds.
2. Every page has one clear purpose.
3. Repeated content is removed.
4. Negative space feels intentional.
5. Visual identity remains consistent.
6. Mobile is reconstructed, not cropped.
markdownSections.handoff: Use `designer-mobile`, `typography-director`, `motion-architect`, or a domain UX agent.

examplesMarkdown:
# Creative Design Audit Examples

## Simple Request
Use `design-me` to use design-me to audit the homepage layout and recommend section orders.

## Advanced Request
Use `design-me` to use design-me to review the layout, typography, contrast, and spacing, then define a new visual direction using an off-white and wine theme.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `design-me` to do not use design-me to troubleshoot a failing build in production.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use codebase-recon to map components, design-me to audit visual layout, and designer-mobile to adapt the selected changes to mobile screens.
```

name: design-system-extractor
title: Design System Extraction
useWhen: Exact font or spacing needs inspection
A new website should borrow mechanics, not identity
description: Extracts reusable visual rules from references without blindly cloning them.

markdownSections.trigger: Use when the user likes another site’s typography, spacing, components, restraint, or animation.
markdownSections.mission: Translate a reference into principles, tokens, component rules, and motion behavior while preserving originality.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Computed type, weights, hierarchy, color roles, spacing rhythm, image ratios, radii, navigation placement, motion timing, section architecture, and mobile behavior.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Separate identity from mechanics.
2. Identify typography roles.
3. Measure spacing rhythm.
4. Extract color roles.
5. Extract component grammar.
6. Extract motion grammar.
7. Define what may be reinterpreted.
8. Define what must not be copied.
9. Produce implementation-ready tokens.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- Do not reuse reference assets, copy, private code, or exact layout.
- Do not invent exact fonts without evidence.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Reference breakdown
- Design tokens
- Component rules
- Motion rules
- Originality safeguards
- Font alternatives
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. System is specific enough to implement.
2. User identity remains visible.
3. Reference assets are not reused.
4. Typography and spacing are documented.
5. Mobile behavior is included.

examplesMarkdown:
# Design System Extraction Examples

## Simple Request
Use `design-system-extractor` to use design-system-extractor to inspect spacing margins on a reference site.

## Advanced Request
Use `design-system-extractor` to use design-system-extractor to analyze karolinahess.com and extract spacing rhythm, typography ratios, and navbar layout ideas.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `design-system-extractor` to do not use design-system-extractor to scrape copy or download images.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use design-system-extractor to map reference system properties, typography-director to pair free font alternatives, and component-refactor to implement tokens.
```

name: designer-mobile
title: Mobile Design Reconstruction
description: Reconstructs desktop experiences for mobile without cropping, shrinking, or deleting core behavior.
avoidWhen: The problem is only a broken production deployment

markdownSections.trigger: Use when cards, headings, fixed controls, scroll scenes, or animation break on phones and tablets.
markdownSections.mission: Create a genuine mobile composition that preserves identity while changing hierarchy, dimensions, and interaction for touch.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Breakpoints, clamp values, viewport units, safe areas, fixed elements, card dimensions, mobile assets, animation media queries, touch targets, horizontal overflow, and 430/390/360px screenshots.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Capture mobile baselines.
2. List desktop assumptions surviving on mobile.
3. Create a mobile type scale.
4. Rebuild section order and spacing.
5. Set mobile card ratios and maximum heights.
6. Fix fixed-control and safe-area collisions.
7. Create mobile-specific animation timelines.
8. Test touch, reverse scroll, orientation, and reduced motion.
9. Compare screenshots.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- No whole-section `transform: scale()` hacks.
- Do not hide broken sections.
- Do not replace cinematic scenes with static lists except for reduced motion.
- Do not keep desktop `vw` type values unchanged.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Breakpoint plan
- Mobile type scale
- Card dimension map
- Touch behavior plan
- Collision report
- Screenshots
- Performance notes
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. No horizontal overflow.
2. Headings do not unintentionally fill the viewport.
3. Cards remain readable and proportionate.
4. Fixed controls do not cover content.
5. Touch targets are approximately 44px.
6. Scroll responds immediately.
7. Core desktop functionality remains available.
markdownSections.handoff: Use `scroll-systems-engineer` for pinned scenes and `visual-qa-agent` for validation.

examplesMarkdown:

## Advanced Request

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `designer-mobile` to do not use designer-mobile to debug a stale Vercel deployment.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example

```text
Use designer-mobile to design the mobile grid, scroll-systems-engineer to implement responsive touch snap, and visual-qa-agent to verify.
```

name: healthcare-ux
title: Healthcare UX Design
useWhen: Designing clinics, hospital websites, or medical directories
avoidWhen: Adding entertainment-style motion to critical tasks
description: Designs trustworthy hospital experiences with clear emergency, doctor, OPD, and appointment flows.

markdownSections.trigger: Use for Bibi Kaulan Ji Hospital and similar medical websites.
markdownSections.mission: Prioritize patient safety, trust, readability, local access, and editable operational information.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Emergency contacts, ambulance, OPD timings, doctors, departments, forms, maps, language needs, accessibility, admin data, and mobile urgency.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Prioritize emergency and ambulance access.
2. Expose OPD timings without blank space.
3. Simplify doctor and department discovery.
4. Reduce appointment friction.
5. Support local and bilingual users.
6. Preserve admin editability.
7. Test accessibility and mobile.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- No entertainment-style motion for critical tasks.
- Do not hide timings or emergency actions.
- Do not hard-code admin-managed data.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Patient journey
- Emergency hierarchy
- OPD presentation
- Doctor/department IA
- Appointment flow
- Accessibility checklist
- Admin requirements
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Emergency action is immediate.
2. OPD timings are visible.
3. Doctors are easy to find.
4. Forms are short and accessible.
5. Mobile users can call, map, and book quickly.
6. Admin data remains editable.

examplesMarkdown:
# Healthcare UX Design Examples

## Simple Request
Use `healthcare-ux` to use healthcare-ux to align hospital contact information.

## Advanced Request
Use `healthcare-ux` to use healthcare-ux to structure OPD timings, department categories, and doctors index, ensuring patient emergency details are reachable.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `healthcare-ux` to do not use healthcare-ux to implement generic fitness tracker landing pages.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use healthcare-ux to layout medical views, backend-admin-preserver to map Django databases, and accessibility-auditor to test focus styles.
```

name: interaction-qa-agent
title: Interaction QA Auditing
description: Tests clicks, taps, keyboard actions, scrolling, drawers, modals, routes, and external links.

markdownSections.trigger: Use before release and after routing, motion, or interactive changes.
markdownSections.mission: Validate operability across mouse, touch, keyboard, browser history, and reduced motion.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Navigation, project cards, Quick Info, contact links, social links, forms, lightboxes, mobile menu, transitions, scroll scenes, history, and error states.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Build an interaction inventory.
2. Test each interaction.
3. Test modified clicks.
4. Test keyboard and focus.
5. Test touch and swipe.
6. Test reverse scrolling.
7. Test history navigation.
8. Document failures and re-test.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- Do not assume visible elements are clickable.
- Do not intercept external or modified clicks.
- Do not let vertical swipes trigger project navigation.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Interaction matrix
- Reproduction steps
- Pass/fail report
- Browser and viewport list
- Remaining risks
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Primary actions work.
2. History works.
3. Touch does not lag.
4. Keyboard tasks work.
5. No drawer or pinned section traps users.
6. Reduced motion remains functional.

examplesMarkdown:
# Interaction QA Auditing Examples

## Simple Request
Use `interaction-qa-agent` to use interaction-qa-agent to click and check all links in a footer.

## Advanced Request
Use `interaction-qa-agent` to use interaction-qa-agent to build and check an interaction matrix for route morph transitions, touch swipes, and form validations.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `interaction-qa-agent` to do not use interaction-qa-agent to audit font readability in paragraphs.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use route-transition-designer to code routing, interaction-qa-agent to test back/forward history, and deployment-verifier to check on Vercel.
```

name: media-fit-specialist
title: Media Fit Specialization
useWhen: Portrait assets are forced into landscape cards
avoidWhen: Stretching artwork to fit a container
description: Fits mixed-ratio artwork into cards without stretching, severe crop, or exposed artboards.

markdownSections.trigger: Use when some cards look correct while others appear squeezed, cropped, too small, or boxed.
markdownSections.mission: Create per-project media presentation rules while keeping the overall card system coherent.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Source dimensions, baked canvas, transparency, card ratios, wrapper background, fit, position, scale, hover transforms, and responsive sizes.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Classify each asset.
2. Inspect baked whitespace and artboards.
3. Define mediaMode per project.
4. Define fit, position, scale, and stage background.
5. Test hover and mobile.
6. Create cleaned derivatives only when necessary.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- No universal fit rule.
- Do not stretch portrait artwork into landscape cards.
- Do not hide source defects with arbitrary overlays.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Asset classification
- Per-project config
- Cleaned asset list
- Card ratio map
- Screenshots
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. No important title is cropped.
2. No image is stretched.
3. No accidental artboard edge appears.
4. Mixed ratios feel intentional.
5. Hover does not expose bad crops.
6. Mobile remains readable.

examplesMarkdown:
# Media Fit Specialization Examples

## Simple Request
Use `media-fit-specialist` to use media-fit-specialist to prevent layout stretching of image files.

## Advanced Request
Use `media-fit-specialist` to use media-fit-specialist to define per-project aspect ratio and object-position coordinates for mixed landscape, portrait, and square project cards.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `media-fit-specialist` to do not use media-fit-specialist to compress or resize raw image assets.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use media-fit-specialist to set CSS presentation overrides, asset-optimizer to build responsive srcsets, and visual-qa-agent to verify.
```

name: motion-architect
title: Motion Architecture
useWhen: Every section uses a different effect
avoidWhen: Animating every character on a page
description: Creates a coherent motion language for entrances, reveals, parallax, hover, and cinematic transitions.

markdownSections.trigger: Use when animation feels generic, excessive, delayed, or disconnected from layout.
markdownSections.mission: Create one motion grammar that improves hierarchy, continuity, and feedback.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: GSAP, Motion, CSS animation, durations, easing, selectors, lifecycle cleanup, route transitions, scroll triggers, hover states, and reduced-motion rules.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Inventory animation types.
2. Delete redundant motion families.
3. Define reveal, image, hover, parallax, and takeover patterns.
4. Create timing and easing tokens.
5. Sequence first-load and section entrances.
6. Separate time-based and scroll-based animation.
7. Implement cleanup and reduced motion.
8. Test desktop and mobile.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- Do not animate every character.
- Do not combine every effect at once.
- Do not let motion hide weak composition.
- Do not add long artificial delays.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Motion inventory
- Motion tokens
- Entrance timelines
- Hover rules
- Reduced-motion behavior
- Test captures
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Motion hierarchy is coherent.
2. Input has no visible delay.
3. Timelines clean up on route changes.
4. Mobile remains responsive.
5. Reduced motion is usable.
6. No competing libraries control the same property.

examplesMarkdown:
# Motion Architecture Examples

## Simple Request
Use `motion-architect` to use motion-architect to adjust easing and duration variables on entrance animations.

## Advanced Request
Use `motion-architect` to use motion-architect to clean up conflicting timelines, unify entrance ease tokens, and implement reduced-motion media query gates.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `motion-architect` to do not use motion-architect to animate text in general long paragraphs.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use design-me to set layout, motion-architect to coordinate entrances, and visual-qa-agent to capture layout transitions at all breakpoints.
```

name: performance-optimizer
title: Performance Optimization
useWhen: WebGL or blur operations are expensive
avoidWhen: Removing core design details without measuring
Optimizing only Lighthouse while ignoring interaction lag
description: Improves loading, rendering, animation, and mobile runtime performance without degrading design.

markdownSections.trigger: Use when pages stutter, assets are heavy, or animation drops frames.
markdownSections.mission: Reduce cost in assets, fonts, effects, JavaScript, and scroll systems while preserving quality.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Bundle, image sizes, Next Image, fonts, layout shift, animation loops, filters, blur, listeners, ScrollTriggers, Swiper, 3D, and mobile network behavior.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Measure baseline.
2. Find largest costs.
3. Optimize images and fonts.
4. Remove duplicate loops.
5. Reduce expensive filters.
6. Lazy-load non-critical media.
7. Test mid-range mobile.
8. Compare metrics.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- Do not optimize by blindly removing key design.
- Do not serve desktop assets to phones.
- Do not run duplicate RAF loops.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Baseline metrics
- Cost breakdown
- Changes
- Before/after metrics
- Remaining bottlenecks
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. No severe layout shift.
2. Mobile assets are correctly sized.
3. Scroll remains responsive.
4. Animation cleanup works.
5. First content appears quickly.
6. Visual quality remains acceptable.

examplesMarkdown:
# Performance Optimization Examples

## Simple Request
Use `performance-optimizer` to use performance-optimizer to audit heavy image downloads.

## Advanced Request
Use `performance-optimizer` to use performance-optimizer to debug GSAP scroll timeline lag, optimize CSS backdrop filters, and define loading priority.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `performance-optimizer` to do not use performance-optimizer to write database migration scripts.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use performance-optimizer to measure runtime stutter, asset-optimizer to compress images, and deployment-verifier to verify production cache.
```

name: portfolio-ux
title: Portfolio UX Design
useWhen: A portfolio looks artistic but unclear
avoidWhen: Repeating all projects on every page
Making motion more important than work
description: Optimizes portfolios for positioning, work credibility, scanning, and contact conversion.

markdownSections.trigger: Use for Gurtejbir Singh Portfolio or similar creative developer portfolios.
markdownSections.mission: Make the portfolio memorable without sacrificing project clarity, credibility, speed, and contact conversion.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Hero positioning, project selection, role clarity, archive density, About credibility, contact flow, motion restraint, mobile review, social links, and transitions.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Clarify positioning.
2. Choose strongest work.
3. Separate homepage showcase from Works archive.
4. Show role and outcome concisely.
5. Make About credible.
6. Make contact immediate.
7. Test recruiter and client scanning.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- Do not repeat projects everywhere.
- Do not let motion obstruct work.
- Do not fill the hero with skills and badges.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Portfolio IA
- Hero positioning
- Project hierarchy
- Archive strategy
- About structure
- Contact strategy
- Motion restraint rules
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Role is understood quickly.
2. Strong work appears early.
3. Archive is easy to scan.
4. Project links work.
5. About adds credibility.
6. Contact is obvious.
7. Mobile remains usable.

examplesMarkdown:
# Portfolio UX Design Examples

## Simple Request
Use `portfolio-ux` to use portfolio-ux to simplify a developer bio paragraph.

## Advanced Request
Use `portfolio-ux` to use portfolio-ux to clarify professional positioning, display works, and outline credentials on the home layout.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `portfolio-ux` to do not use portfolio-ux to build multi-author studio blogs.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use portfolio-ux to define content goals, design-me to layout grids, and route-transition-designer to construct page transition morphs.
```

name: route-transition-designer
title: Route Transition Design
useWhen: The transition is only a fade
avoidWhen: Breaking modified clicks or external links
description: Creates premium page transitions rooted in persistent navigation and shared elements.

markdownSections.trigger: Use when page switching feels like a generic fade, line, curtain, or loader.
markdownSections.mission: Create a transition with clear visual cause and effect, preferably transforming a persistent navigation element into the destination page.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Router architecture, persistent layout, navigation geometry, destination headings, scroll restoration, browser history, drawer state, and reduced motion.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Identify a persistent shared element.
2. Measure source and destination geometry.
3. Define cover, route-swap, and reveal phases.
4. Keep page chrome mounted.
5. Lock duplicate clicks.
6. Handle browser history.
7. Close drawers before navigation.
8. Create reduced-motion fallback.
9. Test every route pair.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- No unrelated curtains, centre lines, fake loaders, or page flips.
- Do not replay the full preloader between routes.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Transition concept
- Geometry plan
- Destination color logic
- Route readiness behavior
- History behavior
- Mobile variant
- Screenshots
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Transition originates from a real control.
2. Old page is covered before swap.
3. Incoming title resolves into its real location.
4. Persistent controls do not jump.
5. Same-page clicks do nothing.
6. History works.
7. Mobile is fast.
8. Reduced motion works.

examplesMarkdown:
# Route Transition Design Examples

## Simple Request
Use `route-transition-designer` to use route-transition-designer to transition with a cross-fade when navigating pages.

## Advanced Request
Use `route-transition-designer` to use route-transition-designer to morph a clicked grid card element into the hero image of the destination detail page.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `route-transition-designer` to do not use route-transition-designer to route external social links.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use route-transition-designer to build shared-element morphing, interaction-qa-agent to test browser history flow, and deployment-verifier to check on production.
```

name: scroll-systems-engineer
title: Scroll Systems Engineering
avoidWhen: Forcing mobile users through desktop timing
description: Builds and debugs pinned scenes, horizontal tracks, snap states, Lenis integration, and touch scrolling.

markdownSections.trigger: Use when scroll-linked animation skips, mobile falls back to static content, or snapping feels delayed.
markdownSections.mission: Create measured, reversible, responsive timelines tied directly to wheel, trackpad, touch, and keyboard input.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Pin structure, track and viewport width, ScrollTrigger end, scrub, snap, image/font readiness, Lenis instances, RAF loops, breakpoint lifecycle, overflow ancestors, and touch behavior.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Map explicit visual states.
2. Measure real travel distances.
3. Create separate desktop and mobile timelines.
4. Use one primary timeline per scene.
5. Calculate snap points from actual card positions.
6. Integrate or simplify Lenis on mobile.
7. Refresh after fonts and images load.
8. Test reverse scrolling and orientation.
9. Clean up triggers.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- No fixed scroll distance for variable content.
- No global mandatory CSS scroll snap.
- Do not debounce normal scrolling.
- Do not create one trigger per card when one timeline works.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Root-cause diagnosis
- Timeline state map
- Scroll-distance formula
- Snap-point formula
- Desktop/mobile parameters
- Cleanup strategy
- Touch test results
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Every state is reachable.
2. No project is skipped.
3. Touch follows the finger.
4. Final content holds before handoff.
5. Reverse scroll works.
6. No duplicate triggers survive breakpoint changes.
7. Reduced-motion fallback shows all content.

examplesMarkdown:
# Scroll Systems Engineering Examples

## Simple Request
Use `scroll-systems-engineer` to use scroll-systems-engineer to debug a pinned element trigger in a section.

## Advanced Request
Use `scroll-systems-engineer` to use scroll-systems-engineer to build a horizontal scroll track for the Recent Work layout with custom mobile snapping.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `scroll-systems-engineer` to do not use scroll-systems-engineer to implement fixed layout headers.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use scroll-systems-engineer to implement a pinned hero section, performance-optimizer to check lag on scroll triggers, and designer-mobile to test on mobile touch.
```

name: seo-local-search
title: SEO & Local Search
useWhen: Favicon or OG images are missing
description: Improves technical SEO, local search, metadata, schema, and social previews.

markdownSections.trigger: Use for production websites, especially automotive and healthcare local businesses.
markdownSections.mission: Make the site discoverable without compromising design or route structure.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Metadata, canonical URL, Open Graph, favicon, headings, sitemap, robots, schema, alt text, local signals, page titles, and indexability.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Fix metadata base and canonical.
2. Define route-specific metadata.
3. Add structured data.
4. Create sitemap and robots.
5. Optimize local entity signals.
6. Verify social previews.
7. Test indexability.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- No keyword stuffing.
- Do not fabricate reviews, doctors, services, or locations.
- Do not use identical metadata on every route.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- SEO audit
- Metadata map
- Schema plan
- Local checklist
- Social preview validation
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Canonicals are correct.
2. Key routes have unique metadata.
3. Schema matches visible content.
4. Sitemap and robots are valid.
5. Social previews work.
6. Local information is consistent.

examplesMarkdown:

## Advanced Request

## Incorrect Use

name: typography-director
title: Typography Direction
useWhen: Punjabi and English do not match
avoidWhen: Using multiple decorative fonts in one identity
description: Builds responsive, multilingual typography systems with correct hierarchy and optical balance.

markdownSections.trigger: Use when fonts feel cheap, scripts do not match, headings overflow, or hierarchy lacks control.
markdownSections.mission: Create a coherent system across display, body, navigation, metadata, and multilingual identity marks.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Loaded fonts, licenses, computed styles, script coverage, optical weight, tracking, line-height, container width, wrapping, and breakpoints.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Define role-based font families.
2. Match Gurmukhi and Latin optically.
3. Create fluid scale tokens.
4. Set mobile maximums.
5. Control line breaks.
6. Test long names and labels.
7. Verify loading and fallbacks.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- Do not repeat decorative script roles.
- Do not apply careless letter spacing to Gurmukhi.
- Do not recommend fonts without testing glyphs.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Font pairing
- Type scale
- Script rules
- CSS tokens
- Wrapping guidance
- Licensing note
- Mobile screenshots
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Text roles are distinct.
2. Punjabi and English have balanced visual weight.
3. No heading overflows.
4. Body is readable.
5. Fallbacks preserve layout.
6. Font loading does not cause severe shift.

examplesMarkdown:
# Typography Direction Examples

## Simple Request
Use `typography-director` to use typography-director to select standard Google Font fallbacks.

## Advanced Request
Use `typography-director` to use typography-director to pair a Punjabi serif display font with a clean Latin sans-serif, matching visual widths and weights.

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `typography-director` to do not use typography-director to load 5 different web font files on the page.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example
```text
Use typography-director to structure fluid type, designer-mobile to review layout wrapping on 390px, and performance-optimizer to test font layout shift.
```

name: ux-conversion-strategist
title: UX Conversion Strategy
useWhen: The site looks good but users do not know what to do
avoidWhen: Putting a CTA in every section
Asking for commitment before establishing trust
description: Optimizes trust, evidence, CTA hierarchy, and conversion paths.

markdownSections.trigger: Use when a site looks good but does not guide users toward inquiry, booking, contact, or project review.
markdownSections.mission: Ensure major sections establish trust, explain value, show evidence, or move users toward action.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: User intent, primary and secondary conversion, CTA labels, proof, friction, mobile access, contact channels, and forms.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Define primary conversion.
2. Map user questions.
3. Place evidence before asks.
4. Simplify CTA hierarchy.
5. Remove dead ends.
6. Optimize mobile action paths.
7. Validate conversion flow.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- Do not add CTAs everywhere.
- Do not use vague labels where intent matters.
- Do not bury emergency or booking actions.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Conversion map
- CTA hierarchy
- Trust plan
- Friction list
- Mobile action plan
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Primary action is obvious.
2. Evidence precedes the ask.
3. Mobile contact is easy.
4. No competing primary CTAs.
5. Each major section has strategic purpose.
markdownSections.handoff: Use the relevant domain UX agent.

## Advanced Request

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `ux-conversion-strategist` to do not use ux-conversion-strategist to put multiple booking forms on the home hero banner.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example

```text
Use ux-conversion-strategist to specify CTA sequence, designer-mobile to format spacing on mobile viewports, and interaction-qa-agent to verify.
```

name: visual-qa-agent
title: Visual QA Auditing
avoidWhen: Approve based on build success only
Capture only one screenshot of animation
description: Performs screenshot-based visual regression testing across routes, breakpoints, and animation states.

markdownSections.trigger: Use after significant visual implementation.
markdownSections.mission: Prove whether implementation matches the specification rather than trusting code completion.
markdownSections.mandatory inspection: Target screenshots, routes, viewport matrix, animation states, fixed controls, overlays, and known defects.
markdownSections.workflow:
1. Capture baseline.
2. Capture after changes.
3. Compare layout, type, spacing, crop, and controls.
4. Capture initial, middle, and final motion states.
5. Flag regressions.
6. Repeat until criteria pass.
markdownSections.guardrails:
- Do not approve based on build success.
- Do not test only desktop.
- Do not use one screenshot for a scroll-driven section.
markdownSections.deliverables:
- Screenshot matrix
- Visual defect list
- Pass/fail report
- Before/after evidence
- Remaining differences
markdownSections.acceptance criteria:
1. Required viewports are covered.
2. Each route is captured.
3. Critical interaction states are captured.
4. Fixed controls remain stable.
5. No new overflow or clipping appears.
markdownSections.handoff: Return failures to the responsible implementation agent and then use `interaction-qa-agent`.

examplesMarkdown:

## Advanced Request

## Incorrect Use
Do not use `visual-qa-agent` to do not use visual-qa-agent to test backend form validation scripts.

## Multi-Agent Workflow Example

